title: SYPALA 2026: Applications Open for IMANI’s Premier Pan-African Summer Seminar
summary: The IMANI Center for Policy and Education has officially opened applications for the 12th edition of the Students and Young Professionals African Liberty Academy (SYPALA), a flagship programme dedicated to shaping the next generation of African policy thinkers and leaders. Scheduled as a four-day residential seminar, SYPALA 2026 continues its mission of equipping young Africans with the tools, networks, and intellectual exposure needed to advance economic resilience and inclusive governance across the continent. Since its inception, SYPALA has built a strong reputation as one of Africa s leading platforms for high-performing undergraduates and recent graduates eager to delve into public policy, governance, market-based reforms, and leadership development. The academy brings together bright, motivated participants from across the continent, offering an immersive environment where debate, critical thinking, and innovative problem-solving are encouraged. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); About the SYPALA 2026 Essay Requirement Acceptance into SYPALA 2026 is determined entirely by the strength and originality of an applicant s essay. This year s essay topic challenges young thinkers to engage deeply with Africa s policy and development landscape: ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Identify one specific policy or business innovation that can significantly enhance economic resilience and foster inclusive governance in a specific African country. Analyse the current challenge, explain how your proposed solution works, and outline one major obstacle to its implementation. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); This prompt is crafted to assess applicants ability to think critically, diagnose real-world problems, and propose solutions with clear, actionable logic. The most competitive essays will demonstrate strong analytical depth, originality of thought, and a genuine interest in how policy or business innovations can advance economic opportunity and strengthen governance systems. In addition to the main essay, applicants must submit a summary of no more than 200 words , which will be used during the initial pre-selection process. Essays must be written in English, must not exceed 1200 words, and must be entirely original. Clear citation and proper referencing are required wherever external ideas or quotes are used. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Eligibility and Submission Requirements The SYPALA essay competition is open to undergraduate students and recent graduates from any African country. Applicants must include their full name, institution, current level of study, age, and contact information. All submissions must be made via email, using the designated addresses provided in the call for applications. Only original, unpublished work will be accepted. Essays suspected of plagiarism or AI-generated content without proper disclosure will be disqualified. The selection committee reviews entries on a rolling basis, so early submission is strongly encouraged. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); Programme Benefits Applicants selected for SYPALA 2026 will receive a full scholarship , which covers accommodation, participation in all sessions, and access to a world-class faculty. Over the four days, participants will engage with thought-leaders, policy experts, and innovators working across governance, economics, entrepreneurship, and civic leadership. SYPALA s unique residential format fosters a learning environment that goes beyond formal lectures. Participants will take part in group discussions, simulation exercises, and networking sessions that encourage collaboration and peer-learning. By the end of the programme, attendees gain not only knowledge but a vibrant, pan-African network of like-minded young leaders committed to shaping the continent s future. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Why SYPALA Matters Africa s future depends on the strength, innovation, and ethical grounding of its next generation of leaders. SYPALA fills a crucial gap by nurturing young talent at a formative stage, equipping participants with the intellectual framework needed to understand policy challenges and envision practical solutions. Whether addressing economic instability, governance deficits, unemployment, or emerging opportunities in technology and entrepreneurship, SYPALA prepares young Africans to lead with clarity and confidence. Application Deadline Deadline: April 30, 2026 All essays must be submitted by the deadline to be considered. Application can be accessed from here . title: Apply for the Azm Global Leaders Fellowship 2026–2028

This highly competitive, two-year fellowship brings together a diverse global cohort committed to principled leadership, rigorous learning, and positive change across cultures and sectors. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); About the Azm Global Leaders Fellowship The Azm Global Leaders Fellowship is designed for outstanding young professionals and changemakers who demonstrate academic or professional excellence, moral courage, and a strong commitment to service. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Fellows represent a wide range of countries, disciplines, and lived experiences united by shared values and a desire to lead with purpose. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); Why Apply to AGL? title: Women’s Leadership in Peace and Security 2026: Applications Open
summary: The Geneva Centre for Security Policy (GCSP) has opened applications for its 2026 edition of Women s Leadership in Peace and Security , a three-day in-person programme designed to strengthen women s leadership in one of the world s most critical fields. Taking place from 30 March to 1 April 2026 in Geneva, Switzerland , the programme offers participants an immersive learning experience rooted in self-awareness, confidence-building, strategic influence and cross-sector collaboration. With an application deadline of 15 March 2026 , aspiring participants are encouraged to apply early. Women s Leadership in Peace and Security: designed for impact This programme is tailored for mid-career women living in Geneva and working within the peace and security sector whether in international organisations, policy institutions, NGOs, or other related environments. Its central aim is to help women step into their full leadership potential, particularly during moments of uncertainty that demand clarity, courage and strategic decision-making. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Across its three days, the course blends practical tools, reflective exercises and peer discussions led by an expert team of facilitators. The journey encourages women to explore the mindsets and behaviours that define high-achieving leaders, while creating a safe space for honest self-discovery and professional growth. Participants leave with not only new skills, but also a deeper understanding of their own leadership identity. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Learning objectives The programme focuses on five core learning objectives that shape both personal and professional transformation: ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); 1. Understanding high-achieving leadership behaviours Participants learn how successful women leaders think, work and influence. This includes examining common barriers, internal narratives and habits that can hinder progress, as well as strategies to strengthen resilience and strategic thinking. 2. Deepening self-awareness Leadership begins with knowing oneself. Through guided reflection and expert facilitation, participants explore their values, strengths, blind spots and leadership style. This self-awareness becomes the foundation for more intentional and impactful leadership. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); 3. Gaining practical tools for confident leadership The course equips participants with actionable tools and practices to communicate effectively, influence with purpose and lead teams in complex, high-pressure environments. These tools help women navigate challenges with confidence, clarity and professionalism. 4. Enhancing individual and team effectiveness Participants discover methods to elevate not only their own performance, but also the effectiveness of their teams. This includes learning how to inspire trust, manage conflict constructively, and foster inclusive, high-performing work cultures. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); 5. Building strong professional networks Because leadership is also about community, the programme brings together a diverse cohort of women committed to peace and security. These connections often evolve into long-term professional partnerships, mentorships and support networks that continue well beyond the programme. Tuition, waivers and eligibility The programme fee is 1200 CHF for the full three-day course. However, GCSP encourages applications from women based in Geneva who come from conflict-affected or fragile regions and are unable to pay the tuition. These candidates may apply for a tuition waiver , ensuring that financial constraints do not prevent deserving women from participating. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); GCSP reserves the right to accept or decline applications at its discretion, reflecting the selective and purpose-driven nature of the programme. Why this programme matters Women s leadership in peace and security is essential for sustainable solutions, inclusive institutions and conflict prevention efforts worldwide. Despite their crucial roles, women remain underrepresented in decision-making spaces within diplomacy, conflict resolution and global security. Initiatives like the GCSP programme help fill this gap by equipping women with the skills, confidence and networks they need to lead with impact. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(133); }); Participants not only gain new competencies, but also join a global community of leaders who share a commitment to advancing peace, stability and gender-inclusive security. Key details at a glance Programme dates: 30 March 1 April 2026 Location: Geneva, Switzerland Application deadline: 15 March 2026 Duration: 3 days Fee: 1200 CHF (tuition waiver available for eligible applicants) With applications now open, mid-career women in the peace and security sector have an opportunity to invest in their growth, refine their leadership voice and join a community dedicated to shaping a more secure and inclusive world. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(134); }); Application can be accessed here . To know more about such opportunities, click here . The post Women’s Leadership in Peace and Security 2026: Applications Open appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Rosa Trustee Application Pack: How to Apply for Trustee Roles at the UK Fund for Women and Girls (2026)

Can bring skills in grant-making, fundraising, communications, financial governance or strategy. May have private sector experience or governance knowledge. title: 2026 Rainer Bauböck Essay Award on the Global State of Citizenship: Explore How AI Will Transform Citizenship Worldwide
summary: The 2026 Rainer Baub ck Essay Award on the Global State of Citizenship is now open for submissions. Organized by GLOBALCIT , the Global Citizenship Observatory at the Robert Schuman Centre for Advanced Studies of the European University Institute , this prestigious international competition invites early-career scholars to critically explore one of the most pressing questions of our time: How will Artificial Intelligence transform membership, rights and practices of citizenship? This annual essay award honors the legacy of Rainer Baub ck and encourages innovative, interdisciplinary thinking on citizenship in a rapidly evolving global landscape shaped by artificial intelligence. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); About the GLOBALCIT Rainer Baub ck Essay Award The GLOBALCIT Rainer Baub ck Essay Award was established to promote cutting-edge research and fresh perspectives on citizenship and democratic theory. Sponsored through the funds of the City of Vienna 2023 Prize in Humanities and Social Sciences awarded to Rainer Baub ck, the competition reflects his lifelong academic contributions to citizenship theory and democratic inclusion. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); GLOBALCIT, based at the European University Institute in Florence, is a leading research observatory dedicated to citizenship laws, policies, and practices worldwide. Through this award, GLOBALCIT seeks to encourage emerging scholars to shape the global conversation on citizenship. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); 2026 Essay Question: AI and the Future of Citizenship For the 2026 edition, participants are invited to answer the following question: How will Artificial Intelligence transform membership, rights and practices of citizenship? Applicants are expected to focus on the impact of AI on human citizenship rather than debating whether AI-driven robots themselves could claim citizenship. Essays should explore: How AI may influence national membership and migration policies The impact of AI on civil, political, and social rights The transformation of democratic participation through AI Surveillance, digital governance, and algorithmic decision-making The global and transnational implications of AI in citizenship regimes ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Both domestic and international dimensions of citizenship are encouraged, offering wide room for interdisciplinary exploration across law, political science, sociology, philosophy, technology studies, and related fields. Essay Requirements and Submission Guidelines Participants must carefully follow the submission criteria to ensure eligibility: Essays must be single-authored Written in English Maximum 3,000 words Include a reference list (not counted toward the word limit) Use standard academic citation practices Written in a non-technical style accessible to a broader public audience Include a declaration specifying whether and how AI tools were used for research or text generation ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); Submissions must be sent via email to: globalcit@eui.eu Applicants must include: A short CV A digital photo Submission Deadline 31 March 2026 at 23:59 CEST Late submissions will not be considered. Selection Process and Evaluation Criteria A distinguished jury composed of GLOBALCIT co-directors and an expert in the field will review submissions. Essays will be evaluated based on: ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Originality of arguments Intellectual rigor Ability to inspire and engage Clarity and accessibility Concise yet compelling presentation of ideas The competition prioritizes essays that bridge academic depth with public relevance. Award Benefits and Recognition The winner of the 2026 Rainer Baub ck Essay Award will receive: 1,000 cash prize Publication on the GLOBALCIT website Publication in the EUI research repository, Cadmus Dissemination through EUI communication channels Invitation to present the essay at a GLOBALCIT workshop in Florence (14 15 May 2026), either online or in person ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(133); }); This opportunity offers both financial recognition and high-level academic visibility within an international research community. Intellectual Property and Data Protection Participants retain full copyright over their essays. By entering the competition, applicants agree to the processing of their personal data solely for purposes related to administering the contest, announcing the winner, and promotional communication. Personal data will not be shared with third parties. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(134); }); Why This Essay Award Matters in 2026 Artificial Intelligence is already reshaping public administration, governance systems, border control, digital identity, and democratic engagement. As states integrate algorithmic systems into decision-making processes, the fundamental meaning of citizenship may evolve in unprecedented ways. This competition offers early-career researchers a unique platform to: Influence international debate Engage with leading scholars in citizenship theory Publish with a globally respected research institution Contribute to ethical and democratic reflections on AI For scholars working at the intersection of technology, law, and democratic governance, this award represents a timely and impactful opportunity. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(135); }); For more information click here Discover more opportunities here The post 2026 Rainer Bauböck Essay Award on the Global State of Citizenship: Explore How AI Will Transform Citizenship Worldwide appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Apply for the 8th World Heritage Site Managers’ Forum 2026 in Busan, South Korea
summary: The 8th edition of the World Heritage Site Managers Forum (WHSMF26) is now open for applications. Organised in conjunction with the 48th session of the World Heritage Committee, this global forum will bring together World Heritage site managers and management teams to strengthen collaboration, exchange expertise, and promote participatory approaches to heritage management. The Forum is hosted by the Republic of Korea and convened in partnership with leading international heritage institutions. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Event Overview Host Country: Republic of Korea Organisers: UNESCO World Heritage Centre , ICCROM , and International Union for Conservation of Nature through the World Heritage Leadership Programme, in collaboration with the Korea Heritage Service (KHS) and the National Research Institute of Cultural Heritage (NRICH) ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Partner: International Council on Monuments and Sites Venue: Busan , Republic of Korea Dates: 16 23 July 2026 (excluding travel dates) Language: English (with interpretation in Korean and French) ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); Theme: Connection and Communication Participatory Approaches to Managing World Heritage The 2026 Forum focuses on strengthening connection and communication as essential pillars for effective World Heritage management. States Parties to the World Heritage Convention are encouraged to adopt human rights based and people-centred approaches that ensure inclusive participation in the identification, nomination, management, and protection of World Heritage properties. This year s theme underscores the importance of governance systems that are interconnected, transparent, and responsive to stakeholders. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Through dialogue and practical exchange, participants will explore: Sustainable management through inclusive, people-centred approaches Integrated governance of World Heritage properties Research- and data-driven decision-making for conservation awareness Coordinated risk management for heritage and communities Communicating heritage values to revitalize local communities Additional discussions will reflect the diverse World Heritage properties represented by selected participants. Objectives of WHSMF26 The Forum aims to: Promote international cooperation among site managers and coordinate global actions for protecting cultural and natural heritage. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); Strengthen and expand the international network of World Heritage site managers. Advance conservation standards, responsible practices, research, and innovation. Support site managers with guidance to maximize the potential of World Heritage properties. Advocate for policies, strategies, and tools that link sustainable development with heritage protection. Provide participants the opportunity to observe deliberations of the World Heritage Committee, particularly discussions on State of Conservation (SOC) reports. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Why Apply? The World Heritage Site Managers Forum offers a unique platform for heritage professionals to: Engage in high-level global discussions Exchange practical knowledge and field experience Expand international professional networks Contribute to advancing inclusive and sustainable heritage management Who Is Eligible? Applications are open to: Current World Heritage site managers and professionals working within site management teams (natural, cultural, and mixed properties). ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(133); }); Only one participant per World Heritage property will be accepted. For transboundary or transnational properties, one participant per State Party may be accepted. Selection Priorities Priority consideration will be given to: Site managers of properties under Reactive Monitoring. Properties inscribed on the List of World Heritage in Danger. Applicants who have not attended previous editions of the Forum. Selection will also aim to ensure balanced regional representation, gender balance, age diversity, and representation of different types of World Heritage properties. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(134); }); Financial Conditions Selected participants are responsible for their round-trip travel , accommodation, and living expenses in Busan. Participants are encouraged to seek funding from their institutions, employers, governments, or other funding agencies. A limited number of travel grants are available for participants from: Least Developed Countries (LDCs) Small Island Developing States (SIDS) States Parties in conflict or post-conflict situations ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(135); }); Please note that travel grants are not automatic and will be confirmed alongside selection results. Participants must commit to attending the full duration of the Forum. Confirmed participants will be communicated to their respective State Party Delegations. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(136); }); Application Process Applications must be submitted in English or French only . A professional CV (maximum 2 pages, PDF format) is required. Applications must be submitted via the official online form for the 8th World Heritage Site Managers Forum. Deadline: 22 March 2026 at 23:59 (CET) Selection Results Announcement: From 27 April 2026 If you are a World Heritage site manager committed to strengthening participatory governance and sustainable conservation, WHSMF26 presents an invaluable opportunity to connect, collaborate, and lead. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(137); }); Discover more Conference Opportunities Here. title: Equity-Free Funding for Blockchain Startups: UNICEF Venture Fund Opens Applications for 2026 Cohort
summary: The UNICEF Venture Fund has opened applications for its next Blockchain Ventures cohort , inviting early-stage startups from emerging markets to access equity-free investment and technical support. The Fund is seeking blockchain-based solutions that can strengthen accountability, transparency and financing for social good. The application deadline is 10 March 2026 . Supporting Blockchain Ventures for Social Impact The UNICEF Venture Fund focuses on identifying, testing and scaling open-source frontier technologies that can accelerate social impact for children and their communities. For this call, the Fund is specifically looking for ready-to-deploy blockchain solutions that have demonstrated real-world use cases and are aligned with open-source principles. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Startups must be registered in one of UNICEF s programme countries and are expected to show commitment to open data, open code, and transparent development. The Fund encourages women-led companies and young founders to apply, reflecting UNICEF s emphasis on inclusion and diverse leadership. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Selected startups will receive equity-free funding of up to US$100,000 in ETH, BTC, or USDC. They will also receive tailored mentorship to support product deployment, impact measurement, and long-term growth over the 12 18 month investment period. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); Why Blockchain? Recent trends indicate that blockchain systems are becoming more accessible, scalable and cost-efficient. Lower transaction fees, improved cross-chain interoperability, mobile-friendly interfaces and clearer regulatory pathways are expanding the possibilities for blockchain in real-world contexts. Blockchain s core attributes open-source code, transparent and immutable ledgers, decentralized governance, and verifiable audit trails make it especially valuable in humanitarian and development work. These characteristics can enhance trust, reduce operational inefficiencies, and help organizations track impact with greater accuracy. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); What UNICEF Is Looking For The Venture Fund is prioritizing early-stage, deployment-ready solutions that can be tested in three key areas. 1. Accountability and Transparency in Services and Payments Startups may propose platforms that: Reduce operational costs in workflows such as supply chains or vendor verification Provide verifiable proof of impact, whether digital or physical Expand financial inclusion for unbanked populations through secure digital assistance Strengthen accountability through tamper-proof records, audit dashboards and privacy-preserving tools Enable automated, data-driven payments through blockchain oracles Facilitate due diligence for donors or organizations ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); These solutions should demonstrate improved efficiency, real-time reconciliation and reduced opportunities for diversion of aid. 2. New Financing Models and Local Governance UNICEF is also looking for blockchain tools that can transform how communities access resources and funding. These may include: ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Web3-based staking or crowdfunding mechanisms Marketplaces linking financiers to Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) outcomes Tokenization of real-world impact delivery Cryptocurrency fundraising models Community-driven governance or data-sharing incentives Startups must show how their tools could build sustainable local economies or strengthen community participation. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(133); }); 3. Digital Public Goods (DPGs) and Sustainable Business Models The Fund encourages solutions that advance global open-source ecosystems, such as: Platforms for funding and maintaining digital public goods Decentralized bounty systems for open-source contributions Matchmaking tools to connect DPG creators with potential adopters Models for valuating and scaling open-source tools with proven social impact Decentralized governance, storage or resource distribution frameworks Teams may also explore incentives for non-technical community members to contribute to public goods, including design, research, or business-model innovation. Who Should Apply? The Fund is open to startups that are: Based in UNICEF programme countries Working on blockchain solutions with demonstrated use cases Committed to open-source licensing Ready to test and scale their product in real-world environments ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(134); }); UNICEF also welcomes applications from teams exploring new, globally applicable uses of distributed ledger technology. Deadline Applications for the 2026 Blockchain Ventures cohort must be submitted by 10 March 2026 . Early submission is encouraged. Application can be accessed from here . title: United Nations International Law Fellowship Programme 2026: Applications Open Until 30 March 2026
summary: The United Nations has opened applications for the 2026 International Law Fellowship Programme , a globally respected training initiative organized by the Codification Division of the United Nations Office of Legal Affairs. Designed to strengthen international legal capacity worldwide, the programme offers participants a unique opportunity to expand their understanding of public international law under the guidance of leading scholars and practitioners. The application deadline is 30 March 2026 , and only complete submissions received before this date will be considered. A International Law Fellowship Programme Strengthening Global Legal Expertise The Fellowship Programme aims to provide high-quality, in-depth training to qualified professionals particularly government officials, legal advisors, and educators from developing countries and emerging economies. By bringing together participants from diverse legal and cultural backgrounds, the programme creates a collaborative learning environment where ideas, insights, and experiences can be shared openly. This interactive approach encourages deeper understanding of contemporary issues and promotes long-term international cooperation. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); The programme accommodates up to 20 participants , ensuring an intimate cohort capable of meaningful discussion and tailored academic engagement. It is conducted in English or French , depending on the year. For 2026, the full programme will be delivered in English . ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Structure of the 2026 Fellowship Programme The 2026 session will take place at the historic Peace Palace in The Hague, the Netherlands , from 29 June to 31 July 2026 . Over this five-week period, participants will follow a rigorous academic schedule built around two core components: the public international law session at The Hague Academy of International Law, and a series of lectures and seminars organized by the Codification Division. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); For three weeks, fellows attend morning lectures at The Hague Academy and afternoon seminars with the Codification Division. During the remaining weeks, they participate in full-day sessions exclusively organized by the Codification Division. These seminars cover a wide range of topics, including treaty law, international responsibility, peaceful settlement of disputes, international human rights law, environmental law, humanitarian law, law of the sea, international trade, international criminal law, and more. All training is based on interactive discussions , requiring participants to actively engage and contribute. Fellows receive a comprehensive package of training materials at the start of the programme. Certificates are awarded only to participants who complete the programme in its entirety. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Financial Arrangements and Support A key feature of the Fellowship Programme is its financial accessibility. For selected fellowship recipients, the United Nations covers economy-class travel , accommodation , medical insurance , training materials , participation in all programme activities, and the registration fee for the public international law session at The Hague Academy. Participants also receive a stipend based on local living costs to cover daily expenses. In addition to fully funded seats, the programme accepts self-funded participants , who cover their own travel, accommodation, and living costs. Self-funded participants attend the same lectures, receive the same materials, and earn the same certificate as fellowship recipients. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); Who Can Apply? To qualify, applicants must have a legal background with professional experience in international law. Students and individuals without professional experience are not eligible. For 2026, applicants must demonstrate fluency in English , supported by documentation if English is not their primary language. Candidates must also commit to attending the full programme. Applications require a completed application form, a Letter of Transmission signed by a senior official from the nominating institution, and evidence of English proficiency when applicable. Two versions of the application must be submitted: a signed scanned copy and an editable digital version. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Application Details and Deadline Applications must be submitted by email to the programme administrators. The deadline is 30 March 2026 , and incomplete or late submissions will not be reviewed. Selection considers professional qualifications, relevance to current duties, gender balance, geographic diversity, and representation from countries with fewer recent recipients. Only successful candidates will be notified. Applications can be accessed from here . To know more about such opportunities, click here . title: Apply for the PhD Position in Gender Studies 2026 at Lund University
summary: If you are passionate about gender studies, feminist scholarship, and critical research that explores contemporary social issues, the PhD-Student in Gender Studies position at Lund University is an excellent opportunity to launch your academic career. Located in Lund, Sweden, Lund University invites applications for a fully funded four-year doctoral studentship to conduct independent research within the interdisciplinary project Gender Struggles in the New Conjuncture . This doctoral position offers emerging scholars the chance to engage with leading researchers and contribute to an important project that examines how feminist and LGBTQ+ movements interact with anti-gender movements in Europe. The role combines rigorous research with participation in an interdisciplinary academic community known for its critical, postcolonial, and intersectional approaches. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); About the Position Lund University, one of Sweden s oldest and most distinguished institutions, is accepting applications for a PhD Student in Gender Studies to join the the New Conjuncture research project. These movements influence political, social, and cultural landscapes, shaping debates on democracy, human rights, sexuality, and public policy. The project seeks to understand the tactics, agendas, and social visions of both progressive and conservative gender politics, making this a compelling subject for doctoral research that connects theory with real-world developments. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); What You Will Do As a PhD student, your work will include: Conducting independent research relevant to the overarching project themes. Engaging with interdisciplinary theoretical frameworks and methodologies drawn from gender studies, sociology, and related fields. Exploring both online and offline dimensions of gender struggles, with an emphasis on empirical and critical analysis. Participating in seminars, workshops, and collaborative discussions within the faculty s academic community. The studentship is structured as a full-time position for four years, with the possibility of extending to a fifth year if you take on teaching or departmental responsibilities of up to 20%. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Why This Opportunity Matters This PhD position offers both academic depth and practical relevance. Gender politics and social movements are central topics in contemporary debates on human rights, democracy, and social justice. By joining this research project, you will contribute to knowledge that helps shape scholarly and public understanding of how gender struggles unfold in Europe and beyond. Additionally, Lund University provides a stimulating research environment, opportunities for collaboration across disciplines, and access to international academic networks. Graduates from its doctoral programmes are well-positioned for careers in academia, research institutions, policy work, and related sectors. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); Eligibility Criteria To qualify for the PhD position, applicants should meet the general requirements for doctoral studies in Sweden, which typically include: A relevant master s degree (or equivalent) in gender studies, sociology, or a related field. Strong academic preparation and demonstrated research potential. Skills in methods such as ethnographic fieldwork or digital network analysis, depending on the proposed research focus. Advanced linguistic or contextual knowledge relevant to European gender and political contexts (beneficial, though not always required). ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Detailed eligibility requirements and guidelines for third-cycle studies can be found in the doctoral programme descriptions on Lund University s website. title: Apply for the SOAS Teaching Scholarships 2026

title: Apply for the SWE India Scholars 2026 – Scholarships for Women in Engineering

title: Call for Mentors in Conservation: Empower the Next Generation of Women Leaders with Wildlife Conservation Action
summary: Across Africa and beyond, conservation efforts are becoming increasingly complex, community-driven, and urgently needed. Climate change, biodiversity loss, and growing pressure on natural resources require strong, informed leadership that can navigate both ecological and social challenges. Yet despite their experience and contributions, women remain underrepresented in leadership roles across the conservation sector. The Call for Mentors in Conservation by Wildlife Conservation Action (WCA) seeks to address this gap by inviting established women in conservation to step forward and guide the next generation of female leaders. This mentorship opportunity is designed specifically for mid- to late-career women professionals in conservation who are ready to share their experience, wisdom, and leadership insights. By becoming a mentor, participants play a vital role in strengthening women s leadership pipelines, fostering sisterhood across the sector, and shaping a more inclusive and resilient future for conservation. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Why Mentorship in Conservation Matters Mentorship is one of the most powerful tools for professional growth, yet access to mentors remains uneven, particularly for women working in conservation and environmental fields. Careers in conservation are often shaped by field realities, long hours, and complex community dynamics, making informal knowledge and lived experience just as valuable as formal training. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); For early-career women, guidance from experienced professionals can be transformative. Mentors help navigate career decisions, leadership challenges, and moments of uncertainty while offering reassurance, perspective, and encouragement. At the same time, mentors themselves benefit by reflecting on their journeys, strengthening professional networks, and contributing to lasting change within the sector. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); By calling on experienced women to mentor others, Wildlife Conservation Action recognizes that building strong conservation outcomes depends not only on technical solutions, but also on investing in people and leadership across generations. About Wildlife Conservation Action Wildlife Conservation Action is a Zimbabwe-based conservation organization working at the intersection of biodiversity protection, community empowerment, and human-wildlife coexistence . The organization focuses on landscapes where people and wildlife share space, particularly in regions experiencing high levels of human-wildlife conflict. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Through locally grounded approaches, WCA supports both ecological sustainability and community wellbeing. Its work emphasizes collaboration with local communities, recognizing that conservation solutions are most effective when they are inclusive, culturally informed, and locally led. WCA s priority action areas include: Human-wildlife conflict mitigation Environmental education and awareness Climate-smart livelihoods Applied research and monitoring Conservation leadership ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); These focus areas reflect the organization s belief that long-term conservation success relies on strong leadership, shared knowledge, and community participation. The Call for Mentors in Conservation builds directly on this philosophy by placing leadership development and mentorship at the center of conservation practice. Who This Mentorship Opportunity Is For This call is aimed at established women in conservation , particularly those who are mid- to late-career and ready to give back to the sector. Mentors may come from a wide range of backgrounds, including wildlife conservation, environmental management, research, community-based conservation, policy, education, or advocacy. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); What unites mentors is not a specific job title, but a willingness to: Share professional experiences, lessons learned, and leadership insights Support early-career women navigating conservation pathways Strengthen networks and solidarity among women in conservation Help create spaces where women can lead confidently and authentically Mentors are invited to lead with purpose, generosity, and openness, recognizing the value of both successes and challenges in shaping future leaders. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(133); }); The Impact of Becoming a Mentor By participating in this mentorship initiative, women in conservation contribute to more than individual career development. They help: Retain talented women within the conservation sector Build confidence and leadership capacity among emerging professionals Transfer knowledge that is rarely captured in formal training Challenge gender imbalances in conservation leadership Mentorship strengthens the sector as a whole by ensuring that experience, skills, and institutional knowledge are not lost, but passed on to those who will carry conservation work forward. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(134); }); How to Apply Women interested in becoming mentors are invited to apply through the official online form. The form allows applicants to share their background, experience, and motivation for joining the mentorship initiative. Apply to become a mentor with this google form . For more information about Wildlife Conservation Action s work and conservation focus areas, visit their website . Looking for more opportunities? title: SIDS Scholarships 2026 – Fully Funded MSc in Water and Sustainable Development for Small Island Developing States
summary: For early-career water professionals and sustainability leaders from Small Island Developing States (SIDS), the SIDS Scholarships at IHE Delft represent an exceptional opportunity to pursue advanced study in water and sustainable development while gaining the knowledge, expertise, and network needed to tackle pressing water challenges in their home regions. Offered by the IHE Delft Institute for Water Education under its Water and Development Partnership Programme and funded by the Dutch Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the SIDS Scholarships provide a limited number of full and partial awards for the MSc Programme in Water and Sustainable Development . These scholarships support graduate study in the Netherlands, including tuition, living costs, travel, and research components that are relevant to the community and environmental needs of SIDS countries. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Scholarship Overview The SIDS Scholarships are part of IHE Delft s broader effort to build capacity and nurture a global network of water professionals equipped to address sustainable development challenges in vulnerable regions. The programme specifically targets nationals of SIDS nations especially impacted by climate change, sea-level rise, limited freshwater resources, and environmental vulnerability to equip them with advanced technical skills in water management, sustainable development, and research. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Full scholarships include: Full tuition coverage for the MSc in Water and Sustainable Development. Health insurance for the duration of the programme. International travel costs to and from the Netherlands. Monthly living allowance of approximately 1,220 per month for accommodation and living costs throughout the study period. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); Partial scholarships may also be available to support candidates who already have partial funding from other sources such as employers, personal sponsorships, or complementary awards. Applicants with partial funding can apply for assistance to cover the remaining costs of their degree. About the MSc in Water and Sustainable Development The Scholarship is open to applicants who have been admitted to IHE Delft s Master s programme in Water and Sustainable Development , which integrates interdisciplinary approaches to water science, policy, and sustainability. The MSc offers both a regular track (typically one year) and a research variant (typically two years), tailored to the academic and professional aspirations of students. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Students enrolled in this programme engage with themes such as water governance, environmental systems, sustainable infrastructure, and community resilience. Why This Scholarship Matters Small Island Developing States face some of the most urgent water-related challenges in the world, including vulnerability to climate change, limited freshwater resources, and infrastructure pressures. Investing in local water professionals empowers these countries to lead their own development agendas, design effective water strategies, and strengthen adaptive capacity. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); By supporting skilled individuals from within SIDS communities, the scholarship not only builds technical expertise but also fosters leadership and regional collaboration. Graduates return equipped to influence water policy, advance sustainable infrastructure, and contribute to national and regional development goals. As water systems become increasingly intertwined with public health, food production, economic stability, and climate resilience, this scholarship bridges education and real-world impact. Who Is Eligible To qualify for the SIDS Scholarships, applicants must: Be national of one of the eligible Small Island Developing States such as Belize, Fiji, Jamaica, Maldives, Papua New Guinea, Samoa, Solomon Islands, Timor-Leste, Vanuatu, and other listed countries. Have academic admission to the regular or research MSc programme in Water and Sustainable Development at IHE Delft. Be living and/or working in an eligible SIDS country. Agree to conduct their MSc research in their home country or region in association with a project supported by the programme. Participate in and contribute to seminars organized by the programme s learning networks. Commit to returning to their home country or another priority country after their studies to apply their skills and knowledge locally. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Selection Criteria Selection prioritizes candidates with: Strong academic background and demonstrated potential for leadership in water and sustainability. High motivation , which applicants express through intentional responses to motivation questions in the application process. Relevant work experience in water management, environmental planning, or sustainable development. A strong case for co-funding , when equally qualified candidates are compared. A gender balance emphasis , with priority given to female applicants. In addition to eligibility, one of the required recommendation letters should be from a current or previous employer, articulating how the candidate s studies would benefit the organization and confirming the intention to return after graduation. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(133); }); How to Apply To be considered for the SIDS Scholarship, applicants must first apply for admission to the MSc Programme in Water and Sustainable Development at IHE Delft. There is no separate scholarship application; eligible candidates are automatically assessed for scholarship consideration once they have received their academic admission letter. Preparation should include: Academic transcripts and degree certificates English proficiency results (if required; some SIDS applicants may be exempt) Two letters of recommendation (including an employer recommendation) A detailed motivation statement addressing program priorities and personal goals Application deadline: 1 June 2025 For detailed information and to begin your application, visit the official SIDS Scholarships page on the IHE Delft website. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(134); }); Looking for more opportunities? title: 3rd Global MAB Youth Forum 2026 — Fully Funded UNESCO Youth Forum in Italy (Apply Now)
summary: Key Details Organizer: UNESCO Man and the Biosphere (MAB) Programme Location: Rosolina Mare, Italy Dates: 18 22 May 2026 Format: In-person Age range: 18 35 years Maximum participants: 200 Theme: Committed to the Future We Want Funding: Accommodation and meals covered; limited travel support available Application deadline: 23 March 2026 (23:59 CET) Contact: mab@unesco.org The third Global MAB Youth Forum marks a key milestone for strengthening youth participation in UNESCO s Man and the Biosphere (MAB) Programme. UNESCO 18 May 2026 22 May 2026 Theme Building on the 2017 and 2019 Global MAB Youth Forums, the third forum adopts the theme Committed to the Future We Want. Facilitate the exchange of youth-led practices and initiatives in Biosphere Reserves related to biodiversity conservation, sustainable development, and community well-being. In view of their official national designation, National MAB Youth Focal Points who submit the required documentation shall be eligible to participate in the Forum without being subject to a competitive selection process. MAB Youth Participants (Open Call Other than National MAB Youth Focal Points): Eligible young people aged 18 35 who are actively engaged in or connected to UNESCO Biosphere Reserves (e.g., living, working, studying, volunteering, conducting research, or supporting BR-related activities) may apply through an open Expression of Interest process. A brief statement (max. 250 words) explaining how they plan to follow up at national or regional levels after the Forum. An indication of whether they have confirmed financial support to cover their travel costs. If yes, supporting documentation may be uploaded. APPLY MAB Youth Participants (open call) Applicants are requested to submit the following: A recent Curriculum Vitae (CV). A short description of their experience, initiatives, or engagement related to UNESCO Biosphere Reserves, including their role and key outcomes. (Max. 250 words) A statement outlining the contributions they intend to bring to the Forum and the outcomes they hope to achieve. (Max. 250 words) A brief explanation of how they plan to apply the outcomes of the Forum in their Biosphere Reserve, community, or country after the event. (Max. 250 words) A letter of endorsement or support from a Biosphere Reserve or respective MAB National Committee or National Commission for UNESCO. An indication of whether they have confirmed financial support to cover their travel costs. If yes, supporting documentation may be uploaded. title: Apply now for the PhD Programme in Economics at the Cologne Graduate School (CGS), University of Cologne

title: Apply Now: €50,000 Funding for SMEs Developing Space-Enabled Environmental Solutions.

title: European Ombudsman Traineeships 2026: Paid EU Governance Opportunity for Graduates
summary: If you re an ambitious university graduate or a student in your final year seeking world-class professional experience in European governance, transparency, public administration, or EU affairs this is one of the most sought-after opportunities within EU institutions for career-minded individuals wanting to work at the heart of EU democratic accountability and public service. Hosting Institution European Ombudsman Overview: What Is the European Ombudsman Traineeship? Digital communication Case-handling Traineeship for persons with disabilities Candidates should be nationals of a Member State of the European Union No previous paid traineeship or work contract in a European institution Bachelors degree as minimum, solid training on EU law ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Deadline: 13 and 31 March Location: Brussels or Strasbourg Compensation: approx. 1400/m The European Ombudsman Traineeship Programme is a paid, full-time, one-year internship designed for recent university graduates who want hands-on experience inside a core European Union institution. The programme offers a unique opportunity to work directly on issues related to EU administration, transparency, accountability, citizens rights, and good governance. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Trainees are placed in either Brussels or Strasbourg, depending on the needs of the Office, and work under the direct supervision of senior EU officials, usually a Head of Unit or equivalent. The traineeship combines professional responsibility, structured supervision, mentoring, and exposure to the daily functioning of EU institutions. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); This programme is particularly attractive for candidates interested in: EU law and policy Public administration Human rights and accountability European governance and institutional relations Transparency and citizens complaints mechanisms Aim of the Traineeship The traineeship is intended to: Provide practical, real-world experience within an EU institution Allow trainees to build on academic knowledge through professional tasks Develop skills in policy analysis, administration, communication, and institutional work Promote understanding of how EU institutions interact with citizens ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Each trainee works under: A line manager who defines objectives A mentor who supports daily integration and work A structured evaluation system (mid-term and final assessment) Duration of the Traineeship Standard duration: 12 months (1 year) Extension: Not possible Exceptional cases: Shorter traineeships may be offered in the interest of the service ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); Start date is typically 1 September each year. Who Can Apply? (Eligibility Criteria) 1. Nationality Applicants must be: Nationals of an EU Member State, OR Nationals of an EU accession or candidate country Eligible regions: Europe (EU + EU candidate/accession countries) 2. Educational Qualifications Applicants must: Hold a minimum 3-year university degree (Bachelor s level or equivalent) Degree must be in a discipline specified in the relevant traineeship call ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Fields commonly accepted include (depending on the call): Law Political Science European Studies International Relations Public Administration Social Sciences Communication and related disciplines 3. Language Requirements Applicants must have: Thorough knowledge of one EU official language, and Very good knowledge of another EU official language ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(133); }); English is mandatory, as it is the main working language of the European Ombudsman. 4. Prior EU Experience Restrictions Applicants must NOT have: Completed more than 8 consecutive weeks (or 2 months) of paid traineeship in any EU institution Held any paid employment (temporary, contract, auxiliary, interim agent, SNE, MEP assistant, etc.) in an EU institution ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(134); }); This rule ensures fair access for first-time EU trainees. Special Programme: Traineeships for Persons with Disabilities The European Ombudsman runs a dedicated positive-action traineeship programme for candidates with disabilities. Key Features Separate annual call (usually February) Candidates may apply simultaneously to general traineeships if eligible Open to: University graduates, or Students in at least their third year of higher education Additional Support Reasonable accommodation at the workplace Additional financial support of up to 50% of the monthly grant to cover disability-related costs ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(135); }); Disability-related income does not reduce the traineeship grant Applicants must provide official proof of disability in an EU official language. Selection Procedure Application Timeline Call published: Normally February Start date: 1 September Final results communicated: By 30th June ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(136); }); Required Application Documents All applications must be submitted via the online application system and include: Completed online application form CV in English Copies of diplomas/degrees (no certification or translation required) One-page motivation letter in English (required for some profiles) Incomplete applications are automatically rejected. Selection Stages Eligibility and profile screening Written test (if applicable) Interview (video call or phone) Selection is merit-based, with attention to: Qualifications Skills Motivation When candidates are equally qualified, the Office promotes: Geographical balance Gender equality Inclusion of persons with disabilities Financial Benefits and Allowances Monthly Grant Equivalent to 25% of the basic salary of an AD6 Step 1 EU official Adjusted by: Country weighting (Brussels or Strasbourg) Household allowance (if applicable) The grant may be: Reduced if the trainee receives other financial support Adjusted due to salary indexation Additional Financial Support Case-by-case lump-sum assistance may be granted Extra disability-related costs covered up to 50% of the monthly grant ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(137); }); Travel Cost Reimbursement The Ombudsman reimburses travel costs at the beginning and end of the traineeship if not covered elsewhere. Covered Travel Modes Second-class train Bus or car-sharing Economy-class flight (for journeys over 500 km or sea crossings) Private car (kilometric allowance) Tickets are usually booked by the Office Up to 50 kg of luggage may be reimbursed (with approval) Insurance Coverage Full sickness and accident insurance provided Optional insurance for: Spouse Children (at the trainee s expense) Leave and Working Conditions 2 working days of leave per month No cash compensation for unused leave Sick leave: Medical certificate required after 3 days Hybrid work rules apply Strict confidentiality and ethics obligations Supervision, Mentoring, and Evaluation Initial objective-setting meeting Mid-term performance review (6 months) Final evaluation meeting Dedicated mentor throughout the traineeship Exit interview with Human Resources Termination or Suspension Either party may terminate the traineeship One-month notice required for voluntary resignation Grant payments stop during suspension Due process applies in case of early termination Legal Status of Trainees Trainees are not EU officials or staff members No automatic employment rights after completion ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(138); }); Data Protection All personal data is processed under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, ensuring high standards of privacy and data protection. How to Apply Applications must be submitted online via the official European Ombudsman website once the call is published (normally in February). Important: Applicants may reapply in future years if unsuccessful. Apply here Key Deadlines (Typical Cycle) Call opens: February Application deadline: 13 and 31 March Interviews/tests: Spring Final decisions: By 30 June Traineeship starts: 1 September Why This Traineeship Is Highly Valuable Paid, long-term EU institutional experience Direct exposure to EU governance and citizens rights Strong CV value for EU careers, NGOs, public policy, and law Inclusive programme with strong disability support Work in Brussels or Strasbourg, the heart of Europe The European Ombudsman Traineeship is more than just an internship it s a gateway to public service, advancing democratic accountability and citizen rights within the EU. You ll gain practical EU-level work experience, expand your professional network, and earn credentials that can propel careers in governance, policy, law, human rights, and international relations. title: World Heritage Young Professionals Forum 2026 (Fully Funded) — Call for Applications in South Korea
summary: Overview Applications are now open for the World Heritage Young Professionals Forum 2026 , a fully funded international programme organized within the framework of the UNESCO World Heritage Education Programme . Marshall , the program equips fellows with the intellectual framework and practical tools needed to address complex global challenges while preserving the principles of free enterprise, limited government, individual freedom, traditional American values, and a strong national defense. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); The 2025 George C. Marshall Fellowship Mission The mission of the George C. Marshall Fellows program is to provide exceptional conservative professionals with the opportunity to: Apply the principles of grand strategy. Integrate all instruments of national power (diplomatic, informational, military, and economic). Develop practical approaches to achieving U.S. national security objectives. Strengthen leadership capabilities grounded in conservative policy principles. Vision The program offers a comprehensive overview of grand strategy theory and its practical application to today s global challenges. It is designed for individuals early in their careers who are likely to take on significant responsibilities in advancing U.S. national security. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); Participants gain: Exposure to leading national security experts. Program Launch April 30 (5 p.m. 7 p.m.) Dinner and discussion with a guest speaker. title: Open Now: Truist Foundation Grant for Nonprofits Building Career Pathways and Stronger Small Businesses
summary: The Truist Foundation is committed to building stronger communities through strategic philanthropy and partnerships. By investing in innovative nonprofit organisations, the Foundation supports programs and initiatives that create greater opportunity for adults especially in the areas of economic mobility and small business development. If your organisation is working to empower communities through sustainable, high-impact initiatives, the Truist Foundation grant program may be a valuable funding opportunity. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Areas of Focus The Truist Foundation prioritises projects and programs that align with two key pillars: 1. Building Career Pathways to Economic Mobility The Foundation supports initiatives that help adults gain skills, training, and access to opportunities that improve their economic outcomes and long-term financial stability. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); 2. Strengthening Small Businesses Funding is available for programs that help small businesses start, grow, and thrive especially those that contribute to community development and job creation. Types of Projects Funded The Foundation focuses on sustainable, one-time investments rather than ongoing operational expenses. Examples of eligible funding needs include: ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); Launch of new programs Curriculum development or expansion Equipment required to deliver programs Capital needs that directly support program delivery Capital Campaign Consideration Capital campaign requests may be eligible if: The project aligns with Truist Foundation s mission and priorities At least 60% of the fundraising goal has already been secured through donations and pledges Grant Cycles and Deadlines The Truist Foundation offers three annual grant cycles : March 31 Applicants notified in July July 31 Applicants notified in November November 30 Applicants notified in April Organisations are encouraged to plan their applications accordingly to align with these review periods. Why Apply for a Truist Foundation Grant? By partnering with the Truist Foundation, organisations gain: Funding for impactful, sustainable programs Support for community-driven economic development Strategic partnership with a mission-driven philanthropic institution Access to evaluation and learning resources to strengthen program outcomes How to Apply Nonprofit organisations that serve adults and align with the Foundation s focus areas can apply through the Truist Foundation s online grant portal. Application Steps: Submit your application via the online portal Deadline: 31st March 2026 After You Receive a Grant Successful applicants will be guided through the next steps of the partnership process. These include: Required Actions Sign and return the Grant Agreement via DocuSign Submit payment information through the Benevity secure portal Complete Monitoring, Evaluation, and Learning (MEL) reports on a quarterly, bi-annual, or annual basis depending on grant size Grantees will receive reminders and guidance for each reporting requirement. Additional Resources for Grantees Recipients will have access to a range of support materials, including: Grantee Guidebook Reporting requirements and timelines Grant acknowledgement guidance Payment submission instructions For any additional support, grantees can reach out to the Foundation through the provided contact channels. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Final Thoughts The Truist Foundation grant program represents a powerful opportunity for nonprofits working to improve economic mobility and empower small businesses. If your organisation is developing innovative, sustainable solutions that uplift communities, consider applying in one of the upcoming grant cycles. Start preparing your application today and join a growing network of organisations building a better future for all. title: Argentina Internship Program Summer 2026: A Fully Funded Global Experience for STEM Students in Data, AI & Operations
summary: The Argentina Internship Program Summer 2026 is a fully funded, immersive international internship designed for ambitious students who want to turn academic knowledge into real-world, career-ready experience. Running from June 15 to August 14, 2026 , this 9-week summer internship places selected participants inside Argentina s dynamic energy, technology, and industrial ecosystem. With structured projects in Data Analytics, Artificial Intelligence, Process Improvement, Industrial Technology, and Operational Optimization , this program is especially relevant for U.S. STEM students seeking hands-on exposure to complex, high-impact environments beyond the classroom. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Applications are reviewed on a rolling basis , meaning early applications have a stronger chance of selection. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); Why the Argentina Internship Program Stands Out This is not a shadowing or observation-based internship. Participants work on real projects, meet real deadlines, and are expected to deliver real results. From day one, interns are immersed in professional teams where ownership, feedback, and accountability matter. The program is fully funded , covering: International flights Accommodation Daily meals Health insurance Immigration and visa support In-country transportation While the internship is unpaid, the value lies in the depth of experience, global exposure, and the ability to leave with measurable, provable skills rather than just a line on a CV. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); Areas of Experience Participants may be placed across multiple functional areas, including: Engineering (Upstream and Downstream Operations) Technology, Data Artificial Intelligence Industrial Operations Process Optimization Finance Economics Sales Marketing Energy Transition Placements are carefully matched based on each participant s academic background, interests, and career goals. Meet the Companies Interns will work with leading organizations operating at scale in high-complexity environments, including: Aeropuertos Argentina , managing major airport operations where logistics, safety, and efficiency intersect daily CGC , a key player in Argentina s hydrocarbon exploration and production Honeywell , delivering advanced solutions across aerospace, automation, and industrial systems Tecpetrol , leading energy development across the region Tenaris , a global leader in industrial manufacturing for the energy industry A global commodities trading company connecting vital resources through infrastructure, logistics, and financing These environments demand precision, adaptability, and problem-solving under pressure exactly the skills global employers value. The True Learning Experience Participants learn by doing. Interns work inside professional teams, receive direct feedback, and collaborate across cultures. The experience rapidly builds independence, confidence, and global mindset qualities that differentiate candidates in competitive job markets. In addition to company placements, interns also: Connect with executives and industry leaders Visit universities and innovation hubs Experience Argentina s professional and cultural ecosystem beyond the workplace Life in Argentina Argentina offers far more than its global reputation for soccer. From world-class cities to diverse landscapes, the country combines cultural richness with professional intensity. Most activities are based in Buenos Aires , often called the Europe of Latin America, with additional site visits depending on company placement. How the Application Process Works Apply online by submitting your CV or LinkedIn profile Complete your full application if shortlisted Connect with the program team for guidance Get matched with the company that best fits your profile Complete final interviews with host companies Prepare for departure and begin your international journey Spanish is required to make the most of the experience, and stronger Spanish proficiency is a competitive advantage. Visa requirements vary by nationality, and full guidance is provided if you are selected. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Important Dates Program Dates: June 15 August 14, 2026 Application Deadline: Rolling basis (early application strongly recommended) APPLY HERE For more information about this apportunity, click here . title: 2026 Funding Opportunities in Europe: BNP Paribas Banlieues Project (France) & ILGA-Europe Fund for Racial Justice Grants up to €25,000
summary: Two major 2026 funding opportunities are now open in Europe for grassroots organisations and community-led initiatives working on social inclusion, anti-discrimination, education, employment, civic space, and racial justice. The BNP Paribas Banlieues Project 2026 in France supports local associations working in Priority Districts of the City (QPV), while the ILGA-Europe Fund for Racial Justice 2026 provides grants of up to 25,000 to organisations led by racialised LGBTI communities across Europe. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(127); }); Both calls aim to strengthen civil society, empower marginalised communities, and build long-term structural change. Below is a comprehensive guide covering eligibility, funding scope, timelines, and application processes for both opportunities. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(128); }); 1. BNP Paribas Banlieues Project 2026 Support for Local Associations in France Overview of the Banlieues Project Launched in 2006 by BNP Paribas and managed by the BNP Paribas Foundation, the Banlieues Project is a solidarity programme designed to support local associations operating in Priority Districts of the City (QPV) in France. Each year, BNP Paribas launches a call for applications to identify and support grassroots associations that combat exclusion and promote: ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(129); }); Access to education Professional integration and employment Entrepreneurship Social cohesion and living better together The project not only provides financial support but also strengthens visibility, institutional connections, and ecosystem integration for selected associations. Since its inception, the Banlieues Project has helped numerous associations scale their impact and gain national recognition. 2026 Key Dates Submission of Applications: 28 January 9 March 2026 Application Review: March June 2026 Decision Committee: June 2026 Fields of Action Supported Projects must align with the solidarity theme and fall under one or more of the following categories: ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(130); }); Access to Education Academic support Literacy programmes Cultural integration initiatives Digital inclusion Citizenship education Environmental awareness initiatives Access to Employment Professional integration programmes Vocational training Mentorship programmes Entrepreneurship support Improvement of Living Together Intercultural and intergenerational actions Strengthening social bonds Regional planning initiatives Ecological transformation projects Community-based solidarity efforts Who Can Apply? Eligible applicants are local associations operating within Priority Districts of the City (QPV) in France, including overseas territories. Associations must demonstrate: Active engagement in combating exclusion Strong local presence Commitment to education, employment access, or social cohesion Clear impact within priority neighbourhoods What Makes the Banlieues Project Unique? Beyond financial assistance, the programme: Promotes supported organisations within a wider institutional ecosystem Enhances visibility among public stakeholders Connects associations with the general public Strengthens long-term development capacity The programme recognises and amplifies everyday heroes working at the community level. Learn more and apply HERE . 2. ILGA-Europe Fund for Racial Justice 2026 Grants up to 25,000 Overview of the Fund The ILGA-Europe Fund for Racial Justice is the successor to the No One Left Behind Fund and focuses on supporting organisations led by racialised LGBTI communities in Europe. The Fund recognises that advancing LGBTI equality requires addressing racism as a structural force and strengthening racial justice activism within civic and political spaces. ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(131); }); Through this call, ILGA-Europe will support up to 12 organisations with grants of up to 25,000 each for 12 months. Application Deadline 13 April 2026, 23:59 CEST Applicants will be informed of results during the week of 11 May 2026. What the Fund Supports Projects must run from 1 June 2026 to 1 June 2027 and may include: Creating or protecting civic and political space Strengthening community organising and solidarity Challenging oppressive systems and practices Shifting public narratives Organising public campaigns or cultural initiatives Mapping or documenting community needs tied to collective action Building cross-movement alliances The Fund encourages approaches combining: Care practices Direct organising Collective public engagement Funding Details Each selected organisation may receive: Up to 25,000 for 12 months Eligible budget lines include: Personnel costs Travel and event expenses External expertise Interpretation and translation Communication and outreach Administrative/core costs linked to project delivery There is no fixed percentage cap on personnel or consultancy costs if aligned with project objectives. Partnership Structure Selected organisations will participate in: Three online partner meetings One in-person meeting in Dublin (6 7 October 2026) Quarterly progress calls Interim report (November 2026) Final report (June 2027) Who Can Apply? The call is open to registered and non-registered organisations in Europe that: Are led by racialised LGBTI people Work for and with racialised LGBTI communities Non-registered groups must partner with a registered legal entity. Applicants must be ILGA-Europe member organisations by 1 June 2026 (or have membership applications under consideration by 1 April 2026). ezstandalone.cmd.push(function () { ezstandalone.showAds(132); }); Funding primarily supports EU-based organisations, with potential limited support for non-EU applicants if additional funding is secured. Learn more and apply HERE . Comparison of the Two 2026 Funding Opportunities BNP Paribas Banlieues Project: Focus: Local associations in French QPV areas Themes: Education, employment, social cohesion Geographic Scope: France (including overseas territories) Deadline: 9 March 2026 ILGA-Europe Fund for Racial Justice: Focus: Racialised LGBTI-led organisations Themes: Civic space, racial justice, LGBTI equality Geographic Scope: Europe (primarily EU Member States) Deadline: 13 April 2026 Both programmes strengthen grassroots organising and community-led change. For more global grant opportunities, visit HERE .
